solution:
g(t)\,dt$. By assumption, $G$ is a strictly increasing, thrice continuously
differentiable function. It is also bounded: for $x>1$, we have
\[
0 < G(x)-G(1) = \int_1^x g(t)\,dt \leq \int_1^x dt/t^2 = 1,
\]
and similarly, for $x<-1$, we have $0 > G(x)-G(-1) \geq -1$.
It follows that the image of $G$ is some open interval $(A,B)$
and that $G^{-1}: (A,B) \to \RR$ is also thrice continuously differentiable.

Define $H: (A,B) \times (A,B) \to \RR$ by $H(x,y) = F(G^{-1}(x), G^{-1}(y))$;
it is twice continuously differentiable since $F$ and $G^{-1}$ are.
By our assumptions about $F$,
\begin{multline*}
\frac{\partial H}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial H}{\partial y} =
    \frac{\partial F}{\partial x}(G^{-1}(x), G^{-1}(y))
    \cdot \frac{1}{g(G^{-1}(x))}\\
    + \frac{\partial F}{\partial y}(G^{-1}(x), G^{-1}(y))
    \cdot \frac{1}{g(G^{-1}(y))} = 0.
\end{multline*}
Therefore $H$ is constant along any line parallel to the vector $(1,1)$,
or equivalently, $H(x,y)$ depends only on $x-y$. We may thus write $H(x,y) =
h(x-y)$ for some function $h$ on $(-(B-A), B-A)$, and we then have $F(x,y)
= h(G(x) - G(y))$.  Since $F(u,u) = 0$, we have $h(0) = 0$. Also, $h$
is twice continuously differentiable (since it can be written as $h(x)
= H((A+B+x)/2,(A+B-x)/2)$), so $|h'|$ is bounded on the closed interval $[-(B-A)/2,
(B-A)/2]$, say by $M$.

Given $x_1,\dots,x_{n+1} \in \RR$ for some $n \geq 2$, the numbers
$G(x_1),\dots,G(x_{n+1})$ all belong to $(A,B)$, so we can choose indices
$i$ and $j$ so that $|G(x_i) - G(x_j)| \leq (B-A)/n \leq (B-A)/2$.  By the
mean value theorem,

\[
|F(x_i, x_j)| = |h(G(x_i) - G(x_j))| \leq M \frac{B-A}{n},
\]
so the claim holds with $C = M(B-A)$.

descriptive_long.solution:
funcposi(vartime)\,d\vartime$. By assumption, $\funcintg$ is a strictly increasing, thrice continuously differentiable function. It is also bounded: for $\variablex>1$, we have
\[
0 < \funcintg(\variablex)-\funcintg(1) = \int_1^{\variablex} \funcposi(\vartime)\,d\vartime \leq \int_1^{\variablex} d\vartime/\vartime^{2} = 1,
\]
and similarly, for $\variablex<-1$, we have $0 > \funcintg(\variablex)-\funcintg(-1) \geq -1$.
It follows that the image of $\funcintg$ is some open interval $(leftbound,rightbound)$
and that $\funcintg^{-1}: (leftbound,rightbound) \to \RR$ is also thrice continuously differentiable.

Define $\funcconv: (leftbound,rightbound) \times (leftbound,rightbound) \to \RR$ by $\funcconv(\variablex,\variabley) = \funcmain(\funcintg^{-1}(\variablex), \funcintg^{-1}(\variabley))$;
it is twice continuously differentiable since $\funcmain$ and $\funcintg^{-1}$ are.
By our assumptions about $\funcmain$,
\begin{multline*}
\frac{\partial \funcconv}{\partial \variablex} + \frac{\partial \funcconv}{\partial \variabley} =
    \frac{\partial \funcmain}{\partial \variablex}(\funcintg^{-1}(\variablex), \funcintg^{-1}(\variabley))
    \cdot \frac{1}{\funcposi(\funcintg^{-1}(\variablex))}\\
    + \frac{\partial \funcmain}{\partial \variabley}(\funcintg^{-1}(\variablex), \funcintg^{-1}(\variabley))
    \cdot \frac{1}{\funcposi(\funcintg^{-1}(\variabley))} = 0.
\end{multline*}
Therefore $\funcconv$ is constant along any line parallel to the vector $(1,1)$,
or equivalently, $\funcconv(\variablex,\variabley)$ depends only on $\variablex-\variabley$. We may thus write $\funcconv(\variablex,\variabley)=
\funcsole(\variablex-\variabley)$ for some function $\funcsole$ on $(-(rightbound-leftbound), rightbound-leftbound)$, and we then have $\funcmain(\variablex,\variabley)
= \funcsole(\funcintg(\variablex) - \funcintg(\variabley))$.  Since $\funcmain(\varfirst,\varfirst) = 0$, we have $\funcsole(0) = 0$. Also, $\funcsole$
is twice continuously differentiable (since it can be written as $\funcsole(\variablex)
= \funcconv((leftbound+rightbound+\variablex)/2,(leftbound+rightbound-\variablex)/2)$), so $|\funcsole'|$ is bounded on the closed interval $[-(rightbound-leftbound)/2,
(rightbound-leftbound)/2]$, say by $\maxbound$.

Given $\entryone,\dots,\entrylast \in \RR$ for some $\countnum \geq 2$, the numbers
$\funcintg(\entryone),\dots,\funcintg(\entrylast)$ all belong to $(leftbound,rightbound)$, so we can choose indices
$\indexone$ and $\indextwo$ so that $|\funcintg(\entryindexone) - \funcintg(\entryindtwo)| \leq (rightbound-leftbound)/\countnum \leq (rightbound-leftbound)/2$.  By the
mean value theorem,
\[
|\funcmain(\entryindexone, \entryindtwo)| = |\funcsole(\funcintg(\entryindexone) - \funcintg(\entryindtwo))| \leq \maxbound \frac{rightbound-leftbound}{\countnum},
\]
so the claim holds with $\constbig = \maxbound(rightbound-leftbound)$.

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
buttercup(pinecone)\,dt$. By assumption, $riverstone$ is a strictly increasing, thrice continuously
differentiable function. It is also bounded: for $firestone>1$, we have
\[
0 < riverstone(firestone)-riverstone(1) = \int_1^{firestone} buttercup(pinecone)\,dt \leq \int_1^{firestone} dt/t^2 = 1,
\]
and similarly, for $firestone<-1$, we have $0 > riverstone(firestone)-riverstone(-1) \geq -1$.
It follows that the image of $riverstone$ is some open interval $(pumpkinseed,dragonfly)$
and that $riverstone^{-1}: (pumpkinseed,dragonfly) \to \RR$ is also thrice continuously differentiable.

Define $sandcastle: (pumpkinseed,dragonfly) \times (pumpkinseed,dragonfly) \to \RR$ by $sandcastle(firestone,lavender) = moonlight(riverstone^{-1}(firestone), riverstone^{-1}(lavender))$;
it is twice continuously differentiable since $moonlight$ and $riverstone^{-1}$ are.
By our assumptions about $moonlight$,
\begin{multline*}
\frac{\partial sandcastle}{\partial firestone} + \frac{\partial sandcastle}{\partial lavender} =
    \frac{\partial moonlight}{\partial firestone}(riverstone^{-1}(firestone), riverstone^{-1}(lavender))
    \cdot \frac{1}{buttercup(riverstone^{-1}(firestone))}\\
    + \frac{\partial moonlight}{\partial lavender}(riverstone^{-1}(firestone), riverstone^{-1}(lavender))
    \cdot \frac{1}{buttercup(riverstone^{-1}(lavender))} = 0.
\end{multline*}
Therefore $sandcastle$ is constant along any line parallel to the vector $(1,1)$,
or equivalently, $sandcastle(firestone,lavender)$ depends only on $firestone-lavender$. We may thus write $sandcastle(firestone,lavender) =
nightshade(firestone-lavender)$ for some function $nightshade$ on $(-(dragonfly-pumpkinseed), dragonfly-pumpkinseed)$, and we then have $moonlight(firestone,lavender)
= nightshade(riverstone(firestone) - riverstone(lavender))$.  Since $moonlight(sailboat,sailboat) = 0$, we have $nightshade(0) = 0$. Also, $nightshade$
is twice continuously differentiable (since it can be written as $nightshade(firestone)
= sandcastle((pumpkinseed+dragonfly+firestone)/2,(pumpkinseed+dragonfly-firestone)/2)$), so $|nightshade'|$ is bounded on the closed interval $[-(dragonfly-pumpkinseed)/2,
(dragonfly-pumpkinseed)/2]$, say by $blacksmith$.

Given $firestoneone,\dots,firestonelast \in \RR$ for some $goldcrest \geq 2$, the numbers
$riverstone(firestoneone),\dots,riverstone(firestonelast)$ all belong to $(pumpkinseed,dragonfly)$, so we can choose indices
$farmstead$ and $driftwood$ so that $|riverstone(granitelimb) - riverstone(marblearch)| \leq (dragonfly-pumpkinseed)/goldcrest \leq (dragonfly-pumpkinseed)/2$.  By the
mean value theorem,

\[
|moonlight(granitelimb, marblearch)| = |nightshade(riverstone(granitelimb) - riverstone(marblearch))| \leq blacksmith \frac{dragonfly-pumpkinseed}{goldcrest},
\]
so the claim holds with $parchment = blacksmith(dragonfly-pumpkinseed)$.}

garbled_string.solution:
mnsdjkan(skdjfhgp)\,dskdjfhgp$. By assumption, $rlekzpwd$ is a strictly increasing, thrice continuously
differentiable function. It is also bounded: for $zxbvclpa>1$, we have
\[
0 < rlekzpwd(zxbvclpa)-rlekzpwd(1) = \int_1^{zxbvclpa} mnsdjkan(skdjfhgp)\,dskdjfhgp \leq \int_1^{zxbvclpa} dskdjfhgp/skdjfhgp^2 = 1,
\]
and similarly, for $zxbvclpa<-1$, we have $0 > rlekzpwd(zxbvclpa)-rlekzpwd(-1) \geq -1$.
It follows that the image of $rlekzpwd$ is some open interval $(woxucvhm,yalripqn)$
and that $rlekzpwd^{-1}: (woxucvhm,yalripqn) \to \RR$ is also thrice continuously differentiable.

Define $nkqwejlr: (woxucvhm,yalripqn) \times (woxucvhm,yalripqn) \to \RR$ by $nkqwejlr(zxbvclpa,mhgtrweq) = oczxvyre(rlekzpwd^{-1}(zxbvclpa), rlekzpwd^{-1}(mhgtrweq))$;
it is twice continuously differentiable since $oczxvyre$ and $rlekzpwd^{-1}$ are.
By our assumptions about $oczxvyre$,
\begin{multline*}
\frac{\partial nkqwejlr}{\partial zxbvclpa} + \frac{\partial nkqwejlr}{\partial mhgtrweq} =
    \frac{\partial oczxvyre}{\partial zxbvclpa}(rlekzpwd^{-1}(zxbvclpa), rlekzpwd^{-1}(mhgtrweq))
    \cdot \frac{1}{mnsdjkan(rlekzpwd^{-1}(zxbvclpa))}\\
    + \frac{\partial oczxvyre}{\partial mhgtrweq}(rlekzpwd^{-1}(zxbvclpa), rlekzpwd^{-1}(mhgtrweq))
    \cdot \frac{1}{mnsdjkan(rlekzpwd^{-1}(mhgtrweq))} = 0.
\end{multline*}
Therefore $nkqwejlr$ is constant along any line parallel to the vector $(1,1)$,
or equivalently, $nkqwejlr(zxbvclpa,mhgtrweq)$ depends only on $zxbvclpa-mhgtrweq$. We may thus write $nkqwejlr(zxbvclpa,mhgtrweq) =
dmsorvpf(zxbvclpa-mhgtrweq)$ for some function $dmsorvpf$ on $(-(yalripqn-woxucvhm), yalripqn-woxucvhm)$, and we then have $oczxvyre(zxbvclpa,mhgtrweq)
= dmsorvpf(rlekzpwd(zxbvclpa) - rlekzpwd(mhgtrweq))$.  Since $oczxvyre(fkdjshla,fkdjshla) = 0$, we have $dmsorvpf(0) = 0$. Also, $dmsorvpf$
is twice continuously differentiable (since it can be written as $dmsorvpf(zxbvclpa)
= nkqwejlr((woxucvhm+yalripqn+zxbvclpa)/2,(woxucvhm+yalripqn-zxbvclpa)/2)$), so $|dmsorvpf'|$ is bounded on the closed interval $[-(yalripqn-woxucvhm)/2,
(yalripqn-woxucvhm)/2]$, say by $qbweirus$.

Given $ksjdhfla,\dots,pqlzmvna \in \RR$ for some $vbnmlsac \geq 2$, the numbers
$rlekzpwd(ksjdhfla),\dots,rlekzpwd(pqlzmvna)$ all belong to $(woxucvhm,yalripqn)$, so we can choose indices
$rtywueop$ and $hgfdxsvc$ so that $|rlekzpwd(mczxopqu) - rlekzpwd(lkaweiru)| \leq (yalripqn-woxucvhm)/vbnmlsac \leq (yalripqn-woxucvhm)/2$.  By the
mean value theorem,

\[
|oczxvyre(mczxopqu, lkaweiru)| = |dmsorvpf(rlekzpwd(mczxopqu) - rlekzpwd(lkaweiru))| \leq qbweirus \frac{yalripqn-woxucvhm}{vbnmlsac},
\]
so the claim holds with $hxvbnclo = qbweirus(yalripqn-woxucvhm)$.}

kernel_variant.question:
Let 
F: \mathbb R^{2}\longrightarrow\mathbb R \quad\text{and}\quad g: \mathbb R\longrightarrow(0,\infty)
be four-times continuously differentiable functions such that

a) F(u,u)=0 for every u\in\mathbb R;

b) |x|^{3}\,g(x)\le 8\quad\text{for every }x\in\mathbb R;

c) for every (u,v)\in\mathbb R^{2} the gradient \nabla F(u,v) is either the zero-vector or parallel to the vector \langle g(u),-g(v)\rangle.

Prove that there exists a universal constant C>0 (depending only on F and g, but independent of n and of the points x_{k}) such that for every integer n\ge 2 and for any real numbers x_{1},\dots ,x_{n+1} one can find indices i\ne j with

|F(x_i,x_j)|\le \dfrac{C}{n}.
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0.  A monotone primitive.

Because g(x)>0 for every x and g\in C^{4}(\mathbb R), the function
G(x):=\displaystyle\int_{0}^{x}g(t)\,dt\;(x\in\mathbb R)
is a strictly increasing C^{4}-diffeomorphism of \mathbb R onto an open interval (A,B).  We must show that B-A<\infty.

Step 1.  Finite length of G(\mathbb R).

For |x|\ge 1 the assumption |x|^{3}g(x)\le 8 gives g(x)\le 8/|x|^{3}.  Hence for x\ge 1

0<G(x)-G(1)=\int_{1}^{x}g(t)\,dt\le\int_{1}^{x}\frac{8}{t^{3}}\,dt=4\bigl(1-1/x^{2}\bigr)\le 4.

Letting x\to \infty we obtain G(\infty):=\lim_{x\to\infty}G(x)\le G(1)+4.

For x\le-1 we similarly estimate

0<G(-1)-G(x)=\int_{x}^{-1}g(t)\,dt\le\int_{x}^{-1}\frac{8}{|t|^{3}}\,dt=4\bigl(1-1/|x|^{2}\bigr)\le 4,
so G(-\infty):=\lim_{x\to-\infty}G(x)\ge G(-1)-4.

With M_{0}:=\int_{-1}^{1}g(t)\,dt we conclude

B-A=G(\infty)-G(-\infty)\le 4+M_{0}+4<\infty.

Step 2.  Straightening the gradient condition.

Define
H(x,y)=F\bigl(G^{-1}(x),G^{-1}(y)\bigr),\qquad(x,y)\in(A,B)^{2}.

Write u=G^{-1}(x), v=G^{-1}(y).  Using the chain rule,

H_{x}(x,y)=\frac{F_{u}(u,v)}{g(u)},\quad H_{y}(x,y)=\frac{F_{v}(u,v)}{g(v)}.

Property (c) says that for each (u,v) there is \mu(u,v) with
F_{u}(u,v)=\mu g(u),\;F_{v}(u,v)=-\mu g(v).  Consequently

H_{x}+H_{y}=\mu-\mu=0.

Thus H is constant on every line parallel to (1,1), so there exists a single-variable function h such that

H(x,y)=h(x-y),\qquad(x,y)\in(A,B)^{2}.

Undoing the change of variables we have

F(u,v)=h\bigl(G(u)-G(v)\bigr).             (1)

Because F(u,u)=0 for all u, formula (1) yields h(0)=0.

Step 3.  A Lipschitz bound for h.

The function h is C^{3} on J:=\bigl(-(B-A),B-A\bigr).  On the compact interval
I:=\bigl[-(B-A)/2,(B-A)/2\bigr] its derivative is bounded; put
M:=\max_{s\in I}|h'(s)|<\infty.  Then for every s\in I, the mean-value theorem gives

|h(s)|\le M|s|.                        (2)

Step 4.  Selecting a close pair among G(x_{1}),\dots ,G(x_{n+1}).

The n+1 numbers G(x_{k}) all lie in (A,B), an interval of length B-A.  Divide (A,B) into n sub-intervals of equal length (B-A)/n.  By the pigeon-hole principle two of the points, say G(x_{i}) and G(x_{j}) with i\neq j, fall in the same sub-interval, whence

|G(x_{i})-G(x_{j})|\le\frac{B-A}{n}\le\frac{B-A}{2}\quad(n\ge 2).

Therefore their difference s:=G(x_{i})-G(x_{j}) lies in I.

Step 5.  The desired estimate.

Using (1), (2) and the preceding inequality we deduce

|F(x_{i},x_{j})|=|h(s)|\le M|s|\le M\frac{B-A}{n}.

Setting C:=M(B-A) (depending only on F and g) we obtain the claimed bound

|F(x_{i},x_{j})|\le \dfrac{C}{n}.

Since such i\neq j exist for every choice of x_{1},\dots ,x_{n+1} and every n\ge 2, the proof is complete.

core_steps: Build G(x)=∫ g so that G is a bounded, strictly increasing C¹ bijection ℝ→(A,B).
Set H(x,y)=F(G⁻¹(x),G⁻¹(y)); the parallel-gradient rule gives ∂H/∂x+∂H/∂y=0, hence H(x,y)=h(x−y) and F(x,y)=h(G(x)−G(y)).
Use smoothness to bound |h′| by some M on [−(B−A)/2,(B−A)/2].
By the pigeonhole principle pick i≠j with |G(x_i)−G(x_j)|≤(B−A)/n.
Apply the mean-value theorem to get |F(x_i,x_j)|≤M(B−A)/n, giving C=M(B−A).

slot1.description: Numerical constant in the decay condition on g

slot3.description: Degree of differentiability demanded ("thrice" can be replaced by any order ≥2)

slot4.description: Choice of reference points ±1 in bounding G; any fixed ±R with R>0 works
slot4.original: 1 and −1 in the integrals from 1 and −1

slot5.description: Lower index in the quantified statement about n
